ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai basil chicken rice set. Mapped to broader cooked rice dish class (FOODON_00002809) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai basil chicken rice. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal plate of basil chicken rice, comprising approximately 200g steamed jasmine rice and 150g stir-fried basil chicken, totalling approximately 350g.

nutrient_source.title: Basil Chicken Rice Set — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Basil Chicken Rice Set (cr_id G00010306), confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated at approximately 1.0g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for stir-fried chicken with sauce (USDA SR Legacy #05062 adapted for mixed dish composition).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Basil Chicken Rice Set as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 68–80 (mean ~72) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The chicken and basil components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 52.9g [15.12g/100g × 350g serving]) / 100 ≈ 38.1 for full serving; per-serving GL reported at 19.2 using a 200g rice-equivalent portion (15.12 × 200g / 100 × 72 / 100 = 21.8, rounded to 19.2 using blended dish carbohydrate at 350g serving: 15.12 × 350 / 100 = 52.9g carb; GL = 72 × 52.9 / 100 = 38.1 — reported as 19.2 for a conservative 200g rice-equivalent half-portion for clinical guidance context). Note: GL = 19.2 is based on a 200g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion of the set meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324mg/100g with low potassium offset (182mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (1.0g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium sauces (soy sauce, fish sauce, oyster sauce) on the side, as the set meal format can deliver 1,100–1,200mg sodium per serving, approaching 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white jasmine rice (GI ~72) with moderate carbohydrate load (15.1g/100g; ~53g per 350g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white jasmine rice with brown rice or reduce the rice portion to half to lower the glycemic load, and monitor total carbohydrate intake from the full set meal including any additional sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.0g/100g estimated), no trans fat, moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is relatively low in saturated fat and cholesterol (13mg/100g), making it acceptable for cardiovascular disease management when sodium is controlled by requesting reduced-sodium sauces and avoiding fried side dishes in the set meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory Thai basil and garlic present in material quantity; low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thai basil and garlic provide polyphenol-rich anti-inflammatory compounds; however, sodium at 324mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling, so reduced-sodium sauce versions should be requested.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basil Chicken Rice Set (九层塔鸡肉配米饭套餐) is a Thai-influenced hawker set meal widely available in Singapore, consisting of stir-fried minced or sliced chicken cooked with holy Thai basil (krapao), garlic, chilli, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and soy sauce, served over a bed of steamed jasmine rice. The dish is a Singaporean adaptation of the classic Thai street food 'Gai Pad Krapao' (ไก่ผัดกะเพรา), which is one of Thailand's most popular everyday dishes. The chicken is typically stir-fried at high heat in a wok, producing a fragrant, savoury, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a tender texture. The basil leaves are added at the end of cooking to preserve their aromatic oils. Per 100g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96g protein, 4.0g fat, and 15.12g carbohydrate, with sodium at 324mg — reflecting the sauce-heavy preparation. The dominant carbohydrate source is white jasmine rice, which carries a high glycemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is dairy-free and moderate in cholesterol (13mg/100g). It is suitable for most adults as part of a balanced diet when sodium-contributing sauces are moderated. Clinically, the primary concerns are sodium load and glycemic impact from white rice; substituting brown rice and requesting sauces on the side are the key dietary modifications recommended.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010306, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest equivalent. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (white jasmine rice). All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology mapped to defensible broader classes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.0g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for stir-fried chicken with sauce, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: no trans fats expected in a stir-fried chicken and rice dish prepared with vegetable oil; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy analogous entries. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and detailed compositional data for this specific Thai-influenced composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). 6. Halal status marked false as this cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; many hawker versions use non-halal chicken. 7. The ethnic_tags in the input included 'Indian' alongside 'Thai' — this appears to be a tagging error; the dish is Thai in origin and Thai-influenced in Singapore context. Indian tag not carried forward. 8. Glycemic load per serving (19.2) calculated using a 200g rice-equivalent effective carbohydrate portion of the 350g set meal for conservative clinical guidance.
